coder_1.comments: The child's primary goal seems to be rejecting eating the toast, but they do this through denial of the proposition that they like the toast (which the mother responds to)

It's unclear what the child means here (especially because the transcription is uncertain), but based on the parent's response with a suggested drink for the child to have my strongest guess is that the child is trying to say something to the effect of 'I don't want to eat/drink more because I don't know what I would want more of.' This would seem to be primarily be rejection of eating/drinking additional food/drink, but it also has elements of prohibition of the parent bringing more food/drink, and these things are communicated through epistemic negation

The child seems to be primarily prohibiting the parent from bringing water, but it is also possible that the child is primarily rejecting drinking water (especially if there is already water in front of them—video would be helpful)

The child seems to be rejecting doing one or multiple of the possible actions that the mother mentions, but since the suggestions are framed as "would you like to xyz" it's possible (though less likely) that the child's primary intention is to deny the proposition that they want to do the mentioned action (especially since they previously suggested that they do want to do the last action that the mother mentioned, i.e. standing at the table)

coder_1.comments: This seems to be an instance of inability—I wasn't completely sure how to categorize the utterance for Bloom, but the primary intention of the utterance seems to be denying that the child is able to do the fixing (rather than rejecting fixing)

The child seems to be prohibiting the parent from not allowing the child to share the toast, but an alternative reading is that the child is denying the proposition that the parent is allowing them to share the toast. The family members' following utterances seem to be justifying why the child shouldn't share the toast, suggesting the former reading.

This seems to be a clear instance of nonpossession for Choi, but I wasn't completely sure how to categorize it for Bloom. The child seems to be primarily denying the proposition that they have received their piece (implying that they should have received it); based on the child's saying "my piece" it doesn't seem that the child is refering to the piece as not existing (to my mind, saying "my xyz" seems to presuppose the existence of xyz)

I wasn't sure about this one. If the transcription is accurate, this seems to be an instance of nonpossession (Choi) & denial or nonexistence for Bloom (with denial as more likely because it seems that the child is primarily denying something about their object & since "mine" seems to me to presuppose the object's existence). However, the conversation seems to make way more sense if the child is saying "not milk" instead of "not mine," with the utterance denying the family members' claims that the child's 'medicine' is milk. It is also possible that the child is responding to some action taking place (corresponding to prohibition, for example). Overall, an audio recording and/or video recording would be very helpful here

I chose normative because this seems like somewhat of a prototypical normative negation sentence (similar to the "horses don't go on boats" example)

If the word is actually "lose" I'm not sure what the sentence would be referring to semantically or how it would fit in with the surrounding sentences, but just based on the sentence structure in the absence of semantic clues my guesses would be prohibition, denial, and rejection. For Choi, another option is failure, but I didn't list this as one of the best two choices because there isn't an indication that the child was attempting to eat the food and then failed to do so.

The child seems to primarily be prohibiting the parent from taking away the bottle (as shown by the child soon saying "I want more bottle"). However, they also seem to be rejecting stopping eating/drinking, and they do these things by denying the proposition that they are done drinking

The child seems to be primarily rejecting having more milk, but their utterance can also be interpreted as prohibiting the parent from giving them more milk. It is also worth noting that in the surrounding utterances the child suggests that they do want milk, so it is possible that the child doesn't understand what they're saying here (or they could have changed their mind or wanted to play some sort of language game/trick)
